title: 3 4 Line Integrals
body:
3.4 LINE INTEGRALS

The work done in moving a particle P from A to B along a path C in a force field F(x, y, z) = F1 i + F2 j + F3 k is given by the line integral Z Z Work = F · dr = (F1 dx + F2 dy + F3 dz) C C

In general, this depends not only on F but also on the path C we take from A to B.

If A and B are the same point, we integrate around a closed curve, denoted by I Work = F · dr. C

Simple properties of line integrals

(i) If C is a path from A to B and C 0 is the same curve except we start at B and end at A, then Z Z F · dr = − F · dr. C0 C

(ii) If the curve C from A to B is made up of two pieces, C1 from A to Q and C2 from Q to B, then Z Z Z F · dr = F · dr + F · dr. C C1 C2

(iii) If ∇ × F = 0 then the force F is a conservative force and can be in terms of a scalar potential φ(x, y, z) such that F = ∇φ. In this case the line integral from A to B does not depend on the path taken and is simply Z F · dr = φ|B − φ|A . C

We have seen in the previous lectures how partial differentiation may be used to analyse vector fields. In this lecture we will see how to integrate a vector field through the concept of a line integral. This arises when we want to calculate the work done on a particle as it is moved through a force field.

In general, the formula for the work done on an particle by a force is Work = Force × Distance where the distance is the distance moved against the force. If the force is represented by the vector field F(x, y, z) and we move the particle an infinitesimal distance

dr, then the work done will be Work = F · dr The dot product means that we only count the distance along the direction of the F. So if we push the object in a direction perpendicular to the force, the work done will be zero. Positive work means that the particle has work done to it, while a negative value means that the particle does work over the length of the path. It’s like swimming with the current (+) or against the current (−).

To calculate the total work done on a particle as it moves along a path C, we add up lots of small line elements dr to give us a line integral Z Work = F · dr C

Line integrals are usually evaluated by parametrising the path and then converting the line integral into a standard integral in terms of the parameter. We will also see that in certain circumstances (when the vector field is conservative) there is a dramatic shortcut to their evaluation. R Example 1 Evaluate C F · dr where F = (1 − 2y)i + 2xj and C is the path in R2 along the circle x2 + y 2 = 9 from (3, 0) to (0, 3) in the first quadrant.

The key to the evaluation of most line integrals is to express the path parametrically with strict limits on the parameter, and then recast the line integral as a simple one dimensional integral in terms of the parameter. The standard parametric representation of a circle of radius r is given by x = r cos(θ) and y = r sin(θ).

F 9π − 3 F

Question: What would the line integral be if we went back the other way along the circle from (0, 3) to (3, 0)?

H Question: If we went all the way around the circle is it always true that C F · dr = 0?

Example 2 Find the work done on a particle traveling through the field F = y 2 i + (y 3 + ez )j + (x − 2z)k along the straight line from (1, 1, 1) to (3, 1, 4) in R3 .

F −7 F

Example 3 Find the work done on a particle traveling through the field F = (yz + 2)i + xzj + (xy + cos(z))k along the circle x2 + y 2 = 1 in the x − y plane from (1, 0, 0) to (0, 1, 0) and then along the straight line from (0, 1, 0) to (1, 1, π).

In this case the path is made up of two pieces: a circular bit and a line. So we will need to evaluate two separate work integrals. We will move quickly through the parametric solution because, as we shall see, there is a much quicker shortcut!

First the little circular bit:

x = cos(θ) → dx = − sin(θ)dθ

y = sin(θ) → dy = cos(θ)dθ

z = 0 → dz = 0dθ. π Note finally that θ : 0 → . Hence:     R
Z yz + 2 dx Z C F · dr =  xz  ·  dy  = (yz + 2)dx + xzdy + (xy + cos(z))dz. C xy + cos(z) dz Since both z and dz are 0 we obtain: Rπ Rπ π

(0 + 2)(− sin(θ))dθ = 0 −2 sin(θ)dθ = [2 cos(θ)]02 = 0 − 2 = −2.

Along the line interval we have:       x 0 1  y  =  1  +  0  t, t : 0 → 1. Thus z 0 π x = t → dx = dt, y = 1 → dy = 0dt and z = πt → dz = πdt. So R R1 (yz + 2)dx + xzdy + (xy + cos(z))dz = 0 (πt + 2)dt + xz(0dt) + (t + cos(πt))πdt R1 R1 = 0 πt + 2 + πt + π cos(πt)dt = 0 2πt + 2 + π cos(πt) = [πt2 + 2t + sin(πt)]10

= (π + 2 + 0) − (0) = π + 2. The total work done is then −2 + (π + 2) = π.

F π F

If the force field F(x, y, z) is conservative then there is a remarkable shortcut available in the calculation of work integrals. All line integrals for conservative vector fields are path independent, which means they only depend on the starting point A and the finishing point B, not on how you get from A to B.

A conservative force is defined as a vector field F(x, y, z) that satisfies ∇ × F = 0. We have seen vector fields like this before and have called them irrotational or curl-free. These vector fields could always be written in terms of a scalar potential φ(x, y, z) such that F = ∇φ. The work done is then simply the change in φ between the start and the end, Z Work = F · dr = φ|B − φ|A . C Example 4 For the vector field F in the previous question, prove that F is conservative and find φ such that F = ∇φ. Hence calculate the work done on the particle.

F π F H H Question: What is the value of C F · dr if F is conservative? (Note C denotes an integral around a closed path C).

You can now do Q 61 to 63
